title: Dice Mechanics
description: Complete system for dice usage, notation, and randomness in D&D

purpose.description: Dice add randomness to the game. They help determine whether characters and monsters are successful at the things they attempt.

dice_types.name: d6
dice_types.description: Six-sided die (cube that many games use)

dice_types.name: d20
dice_types.description: Twenty-sided die (icosahedron) - the most important die in D&D

dice_notation.format: The dice used in D&D are referred to by the letter d followed by the die's number of sides: d4, d6, d8, d10, d12, and d20.
dice_notation.rolling_format: When you need to roll dice, the rules tell you how many dice to roll of a certain type, along with any numbers to add or subtract.

example.meaning: Roll three eight-sided dice, add them together, and add 5 to the total

percentile_dice.method: The common way to roll 1d100 uses a pair of ten-sided dice numbered from 0 to 9, known as percentile dice.

mechanics.designation: One die—that you designate before rolling—gives the tens digit, and the other gives the ones digit.
mechanics.example: If you roll a 7 for the tens digit and a 1 for the ones digit, the number rolled is 71.

specialized_dice.description: Some ten-sided dice are numbered in tens (00, 10, 20, and so on), making it easier to distinguish the tens digit from the ones digit.
specialized_dice.example: A roll of 70 and 1 is 71, and 00 and 0 is 100.

d3.rarity: It's rare to find dice with only three sides
d3.simulation: To simulate rolling 1d3, roll 1d6 and divide the number rolled by 2 (round up).

d20_test.importance: The 20-sided die (d20) is the most important die you'll use in the game.
d20_test.core_mechanic: It's central to the core mechanic—called D20 Tests—the game uses to determine whether creatures succeed or fail at the things they attempt during the game.
d20_test.usage: You roll a d20 whenever your character tries to do something that the DM decides has a chance of both success and failure.
d20_test.principle: The higher your roll, the more likely it is that you succeed.

damage.description: The most common use for dice other than the d20 is to determine damage.
damage.attack_damage: When you make a successful attack roll (one kind of D20 Test), you roll damage to see how effective the attack is.
damage.spell_damage: When you cast a spell, you might also roll damage.

random_tables.description: Occasionally, you'll see a table that uses a die roll to generate a random result.
random_tables.format: On these tables, you'll see a die expression, such as d10 or d100, in the header of the leftmost column.
random_tables.usage: Roll that die, and find the number you rolled (or a range containing that number) in that column. Read across that row for the result.
random_tables.example: The Trinkets table in 'Creating a Character' uses a d100.

percentage_chances.description: Sometimes you might see a rule describing a percentage chance of something happening.
percentage_chances.example: A rule might say there is a 5 percent chance of something happening.
percentage_chances.resolution: You can determine whether that thing happens by rolling percentile dice; if the roll is equal to or less than the percentage chance (a 01 to 05, in this example), it happens.

rounding.description: Whenever you divide or multiply a number in the game, round down if you end up with a fraction, even if the fraction is one-half or greater.
rounding.exceptions: Some rules make an exception and tell you to round up.

narrative_interpretation.description: Part of the fun of D&D is interpreting what die rolls mean in the story.
narrative_interpretation.randomness_value: The randomness that dice bring often introduces the unexpected: an expert fails, a novice succeeds, or any number of other twists.
narrative_interpretation.dm_role: When a D20 Test or another roll produces a surprise, the Dungeon Master often gives a fun description of what happened.
narrative_interpretation.player_role: If you're a player, look for opportunities to interpret your character's die rolls and describe why things unfolded unexpectedly or even humorously.
